examples: Beryllium difluoride is very soluble in water, unlike the other alkaline earths.
Beryl is a beryllium-aluminum silicate, and its color comes from small amounts of chromium.
Isotopes of carbon in tree rings and beryllium in ice cores show a drop-off in solar radiation during much of the period.
When the Sun becomes more active, this isotope becomes more abundant along with beryllium, another radioactive element.
Another analysis, also published in the journal Science, looks at the amount of the isotope beryllium-10 in the lunar soil.
